learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Loops, Conditions, Greedy Algorithms, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine a parking lot where every parked car requires one empty parking space on both sides for safety. Before parking a new car, you must ensure that both neighboring spots are empty. Similarly, a flower can only be planted if both adjacent plots are empty.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine a parking lot where every parked car requires one empty parking space on both sides for safety. Before parking a new car, you must ensure that both neighboring spots are empty. Similarly, a flower can only be planted if both adjacent plots are empty.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this greedy simulation whenever you need to maximize placements while respecting adjacency constraints.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem asks to maximize placements while maintaining adjacency constraints, think of Greedy Placement.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You have a long flowerbed in which some plots are planted and others are empty. However, flowers cannot be planted in adjacent plots. Given an integer array flowerbed containing 0's and 1's, where 0 means empty and 1 means planted, and an integer n, return true if n new flowers can be planted without violating the no-adjacent-flowers rule, otherwise return false.

examples.explanation: Only one additional flower can be planted.

constraints: There are no adjacent flowers initially.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Generate every possible combination of planting flowers while respecting the adjacency rule and determine whether at least n flowers can be planted.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Traverse the flowerbed from left to right. Whenever you find an empty plot whose left and right neighbors are also empty (or outside the array), plant a flower immediately. This greedy decision never hurts future placements.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Plant a flower whenever both neighboring plots are empty.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem asks to maximize placements while maintaining adjacency constraints, think of Greedy Placement.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Planting a flower as soon as a valid position is found never reduces future opportunities. Every plot is visited once, resulting in linear time and constant extra space.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem asks to maximize placements while maintaining adjacency constraints, think of Greedy Placement.
commonWrongPaths: Not handling the first plot correctly.
Not handling the last plot correctly.
Forgetting to update the flowerbed after planting.
Ignoring the case when n equals 0.

hints: Check both neighboring plots before planting.
Treat array boundaries as empty plots.
commonMisconceptions: Not handling the first plot correctly.
Not handling the last plot correctly.
Forgetting to update the flowerbed after planting.
Ignoring the case when n equals 0.

pattern.name: Greedy Placement
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem asks to maximize placements while maintaining adjacency constraints, think of Greedy Placement.

approaches.title: Try Every Possible Placement
approaches.idea: Generate every possible combination of planting flowers while respecting the adjacency rule and determine whether at least n flowers can be planted.
algorithm: Check if at least n flowers can be planted.
approaches.dryRun:
flowerbed=[1,0,0,0,1]
Try every possible placement.
Maximum new flowers = 1.

code.java: Backtracking solution omitted because it is exponential.

approaches.title: Greedy Simulation
approaches.idea: Scan the flowerbed from left to right. Whenever a valid empty plot is found, plant a flower immediately and continue scanning.
algorithm: Plant if both neighbors are empty.

approaches.title: Greedy Placement
approaches.idea: Traverse the flowerbed once. If the current plot is empty and both neighboring plots are also empty (or out of bounds), plant a flower there, increment the count, and continue. Stop early if enough flowers have been planted.
approaches.intuition: Planting a flower as soon as a valid position is found never reduces future opportunities. Every plot is visited once, resulting in linear time and constant extra space.
algorithm: If both are empty, plant a flower.
approaches.dryRun:
flowerbed=[1,0,0,0,1], n=1
Index2 has empty neighbors.
Plant flower.
Count=1
Answer=true

visualization.description: Plant a flower only when both neighboring plots are empty.

interviewFollowUps: Can you solve this without modifying the input array?
Why is the greedy strategy always correct?
What if adjacent distance must be two instead of one?
How would you maximize total planted flowers?
interviewTips: Explain why planting immediately is safe.
Discuss why the algorithm is greedy.
State O(n) time and O(1) space.
Remember to update the flowerbed after planting.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Plant a flower whenever both neighboring plots are empty.

importantPoints: Plant only when both neighbors are empty.
commonMistakes: Not handling the first plot correctly.
Not handling the last plot correctly.
Forgetting to update the flowerbed after planting.
Ignoring the case when n equals 0.
